food_name: Bak Kut Teh (Pork Rib and Prime Rib Herbal Soup)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Bak Kut Teh; 'pork rib soup' (FOODON_03311737) is the closest defensible match. The herbal broth and Malaysian-style preparation are not captured by this code.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for a pork-dominant soup dish; the herbal broth matrix is not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Bak Kut Teh. A0FJP (cooked pork) is used as the primary ingredient anchor. Broth and herbal components are not represented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to Bak Kut Teh as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Bak Kut Teh is approximately 500 g, comprising pork ribs, prime ribs, and herbal broth, as typically served at Malaysian and Singaporean hawker stalls and restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database — Bak Kut Teh (pork rib soup, herbal broth)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Bak Kut Teh (pork rib soup) used as the primary nutrient anchor. The input values (energy 90 kcal, protein 7 g, fat 6 g, carbohydrate 2 g per 100 g) are consistent with HPB-published ranges for Malaysian-style herbal Bak Kut Teh. Values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pork ribs, braised (NDB 10124) and IMR Malaysia MyFCD for pork-based soups. The input per_100g values are retained as they fall within the plausible range supported by these approved sources. Sodium at 360 mg/100 g is consistent with soy-sauce-seasoned pork broth in HPB data.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — analogous estimate for low-carbohydrate pork-based soup
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bak Kut Teh in the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish contains only 2.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g (predominantly from soy sauce and trace herbal ingredients). A GI of 25 is estimated by analogy with other low-carbohydrate pork-based soups and broths documented in the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently fall in the low GI range (GI < 30) due to negligible digestible carbohydrate content. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (25 × 10.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.5, which is very low. This estimation is conservative and clinically defensible.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 360 mg per 100 g; a 500 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to half a bowl, request reduced-sodium soy sauce, avoid drinking the broth, and monitor total daily sodium intake carefully when consuming Bak Kut Teh.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content at 2.0 g per 100 g with sugar at 0.6 g per 100 g; estimated GI of 25 (low); glycemic load per 500 g serving is approximately 2.5.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bak Kut Teh is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-glycaemic accompaniments such as white rice or you tiao, as the dish itself has minimal glycaemic impact due to its very low carbohydrate and high protein content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 2.4 g per 100 g and cholesterol at 20 mg per 100 g from pork ribs; a 500 g serving provides 12 g saturated fat, which is close to the daily limit of 20 g for cardiovascular risk management; sodium at 360 mg per 100 g further elevates cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Bak Kut Teh to small portions, choose leaner cuts, avoid drinking the broth to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake, and balance the meal with high-fibre vegetables to offset the saturated fat load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork ribs are red meat containing arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid) which amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways; sodium at 360 mg per 100 g may worsen RA-related tissue oedema.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit Bak Kut Teh due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of pork; if consumed, request a tofu-based version, use reduced-sodium soy sauce, and avoid drinking the broth.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bak Kut Teh (肉骨茶, literally 'meat bone tea') is a traditional Malaysian and Singaporean soup dish consisting of pork ribs and prime ribs slow-simmered for several hours in a deeply aromatic herbal broth. The Malaysian (Klang) style, as represented by this dish, is characterised by a dark, rich, intensely peppery broth seasoned with white pepper, garlic, cinnamon, cloves, star anise, dang gui (Chinese angelica root), goji berries, soy sauce, and dark soy sauce. The pork ribs become fall-off-the-bone tender through prolonged simmering, absorbing the complex herbal and spice flavours. The broth is savoury, warming, and deeply umami with a pronounced peppery heat. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 90 kcal, 7 g protein, 6 g fat (2.4 g saturated), 2 g carbohydrate, and 360 mg sodium. The high sodium content (approximately 1800 mg per 500 g bowl) is the primary clinical concern, making it unsuitable for hypertension patients without modification. The saturated fat from pork ribs (approximately 12 g per bowl) is a moderate concern for cardiovascular disease. However, the very low carbohydrate content (GI ~25, GL ~2.5 per bowl) makes it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when consumed without high-glycaemic accompaniments. Bak Kut Teh is culturally significant as a breakfast and lunch dish in Malaysia and Singapore, often served with you tiao (fried dough fritters), steamed white rice, and Chinese tea. It is not halal due to pork content.

How much sodium is in Bak Kut Teh?

confidence_reason: The per_100g nutrient values from the input are consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo and IMR Malaysia MyFCD published ranges for Malaysian-style Bak Kut Teh and have been retained. However, the original input source was listed as 'grabfood' (a forbidden primary nutrient source), so the values have been cross-validated against approved sources (HPB, USDA SR Legacy for braised pork ribs) and confidence is set to medium rather than high. No exact HPB entry for this specific restaurant preparation was directly matched; the closest approved-source analogue was used.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for Malaysian-style Bak Kut Teh is not separately reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2026. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this specific preparation is not available in HPB or IMR MyFCD; estimated potassium ~180 mg/100 g was referenced in the input health suitability notes but is not confirmed by an approved source and therefore not entered as a positive value. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported for Bak Kut Teh in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2026. 4. trans_fat_g is set to 0.04 g per 100 g as reported in the input; this is a trace level consistent with naturally occurring trans fats in pork fat and is not a concern at this level. 5. The input listed 'grabfood' as the data source for all per_100g values; grabfood is a forbidden primary nutrient source under Project Veritas v2. All values have been cross-validated against HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (braised pork ribs, NDB 10124) and are within plausible ranges. 6. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for low-carbohydrate pork-based soups; no direct GI measurement for Bak Kut Teh exists in the database. 7. The dish is not halal as it contains pork.
